agent1_snippets.title: Disinfectants -- Definition/Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Disinfectants and antiseptics are active chemical compounds known as biocides, which combat microorganisms on nonliving surfaces and human skin. [1] Biocides such as alcohol, iodine, and chlorine have been used for many years. This activity describes some of the most commonly used disinfectants in hospitals and homes and their effectiveness.

agent1_snippets.title: Skin Antiseptics -- Mechanism of Action -- Chlorhexidine
agent1_snippets.content: Chlorhexidine is a positively charged biguanide at physiologic pH that binds to bacteria's negatively charged cell walls, disrupting microbial cell membranes and precipitation of cell contents. At low concentrations, it is bacteriostatic, but at higher concentrations, it is bactericidal. Chlorhexidine displays a wide range of coverage, including gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, fungi, enveloped viruses, and Mycobacterial tuberculosis . Advantages include its broad-spectrum, fast onset of action, and excellent sustained/residual activity after being wiped from the field via binding to the stratum corneum. It is an extensively used antiseptic in dentistry but at a much lower concentration (concentrations of 0.12% to 0.2% are recommended). [4]

agent1_snippets.title: Skin Antiseptics -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
agent1_snippets.content: In 2017 FDA, issued a rule specifying that particular active ingredients used in over-the-counter antiseptics intended for use by healthcare professionals in a hospital environment or other healthcare situations outside the hospital are not commonly recognized as safe and effective (GRAS/GRAE). [23] After reviewing the previous efficacy and safety data of commonly used antiseptics, an innovative approach was needed. This innovative approach, in turn, would reduce the risk of a serious allergic reaction, enhance clinical efficacy, and reduce the risk of irritation of current perioperative antiseptics. Hence a phase 3 trial was carried out. It demonstrated that a combination of 2% chlorhexidine and 70% isopropyl alcohol preparation was efficacious in significantly diminishing the microbial burden without any major adverse events. [24] [Level 5]

agent1_snippets.content: Anilides possess antiseptic properties and are rarely used in hospital settings. They are primarily found in soaps and deodorants and provide coverage against gram-positive bacteria, with less effectiveness against gram-negative bacteria and fungi. [9]

agent1_snippets.content: For instance, poor specimen quality is the first sign leading to an erroneous result. agent1_snippets.title: Bacteriostatic Antibiotics -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
agent1_snippets.content: There has been a traditional bias against bacteriostatic antimicrobials for a variety of indications, particularly in more acute settings, due to a misconception that they have clinically slower rates of activity and lose efficacy. [1] [3] This perception, however, is untrue in several settings. [2] [1] [3] With appropriate stewardship and understanding of the risk and benefits of these agents, they can be used in a variety of clinical indications to help maintain the efficacy of many of the broad spectrum bactericidal agents commonly in use in hospitals and outpatient clinics. [2] The level of evidence for the composite studies in this article is I-II.

agent1_snippets.title: Skin Antiseptics -- Mechanism of Action -- Isopropyl Alcohol
agent1_snippets.content: Isopropyl alcohol is hydrophilic alcohol that likely causes membrane damage and denatures proteins needed for microbial metabolism and growth. Isopropyl alcohol is typically mixed with water because the microbial activity is greatest in a 60% to 90% solution rather than in the pure form or lower concentrations. Generally, alcohol solutions are thought to have most of their activity against gram-positive bacteria (bactericidal) but may have minor activity against some viruses and fungi. While one of the weaker cleansing agents with minimal residual activity, it has the fastest onset of action of antiseptics.

agent1_snippets.content: Purchasing poor-quality MIC panels can lead to “dehydrated wells” or “mixed wells.” Completing the procedure without personal protective equipment can increase the incidence of laboratory-acquired infections. agent1_snippets.title: Skin Antiseptics -- Administration
agent1_snippets.content: Povidone-iodine scrubs are composed of 7.5% to 10.0% povidone-iodine, typically in an aqueous or alcohol base, and are occasionally combined with detergent. The scrub is painted on a clean skin surface and allowed to dry. The product must dry to be bactericidal with a preferred minimum of 2 minutes drying time, although the latter can vary depending on the product used as the scrub. Clinicians can safely use povidone-iodine aqueous scrubs near the eye and conchal bowl.

agent1_snippets.title: Disinfectants -- Issues of Concern -- Biguanides
agent1_snippets.content: Chlorhexidine, a member of the biguanide family, is an antiseptic with broad-spectrum activity. Chlorhexidine binds rapidly to E coli and S aureus within 20 seconds and is highly resistant to Mycobacterium . The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and the United Kingdom's national evidence-based guidelines recommend using chlorhexidine to prepare the skin before central venous catheter insertion for children aged 2 months and older, as well as for adults. However, there are no specific recommendations for the choice of antiseptic for infants aged 2 months or older. [10] [11]

agent1_snippets.title: Skin Antiseptics -- Contraindications
agent1_snippets.content: Hexachlorophene: The Code of Federal Regulations (CFR-21) FDA (Revised 2022) states that hexachlorophene is contraindicated on burned or denuded skin or mucous membranes and as an agent for prophylactic total body bathing. Additionally, FDA noted that although hexachlorophene is a useful bacteriostatic agent, safety concerns have been reported, such as Letterer–Siwe disease, neurotoxicity, and death. Hence, hexachlorophene-containing products will be considered misbranded and subject to regulatory proceedings. Clinical Studies and regulatory agencies, including the FDA and EMA, agree that benzalkonium chloride is not innocuous, even when used in small concentrations. [18]

agent1_snippets.title: Skin Antiseptics -- Mechanism of Action -- Hexachlorophene
agent1_snippets.content: Hexachlorophene is a bisphenol compound that primarily acts by inhibiting the membrane-bound portion of the electron transport chain and disrupting microbial cell membranes. At concentrations used in skin-cleansing agents, it is bacteriostatic and is primarily only active against gram-positive cocci, with little activity against gram-negative organisms and no fungal coverage. Hexachlorophene has the advantage of having a residual activity for several hours after use and gradually reduces bacterial counts after multiple uses (cumulative effect). Newer agents with broader antimicrobial coverage and fewer side effects have largely replaced hexachlorophene as a surgical scrub.

agent1_snippets.title: Skin Antiseptics -- Mechanism of Action -- Benzalkonium Chloride
agent1_snippets.content: Benzalkonium chloride is a quaternary ammonium detergent (also called a cationic surfactant) that acts by irreversibly binding to phospholipids and proteins of the membrane, disrupting the cell membrane. It acts as a bactericide against many gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria but has inconsistent coverage against fungi, viruses, and mycobacteria. The advantages of benzalkonium are its stability and ability to be applied with a low incidence of irritant contact dermatitis. Like hexachlorophene, benzalkonium has been replaced by antiseptics with better antimicrobial coverage.

agent1_snippets.title: Skin Antiseptics -- Mechanism of Action
agent1_snippets.content: Each antiseptic possesses a specific mechanism of action, the spectrum of microbes targeted, and a side-effect profile that the surgeon should carefully weigh before selecting the agent for the procedure. Other factors to consider when selecting an optimal surgical scrub include the patient's underlying health status, the expected extent of surgery, the anatomic location of the tumor, and the planned reconstruction. Particularly related to the skin, the most common pathogens implicated in SSIs are Staphylococcus aureus , followed by other gram-positive bacteria, including coagulase-negative Staphylococcus , Enterococcus , and group A Streptococci. Other implicated bacteria are gram-negative rods, particularly Escherichia coli and Pseudomonas . Broad-spectrum antiseptics generally cover more pathogens and subsequently are some of the most popular in dermatologic surgery. [3]

agent1_snippets.title: Skin Antiseptics -- Mechanism of Action -- Povidone-Iodine
agent1_snippets.content: Povidone-iodine is a complex of povidone, hydrogen iodide, and elemental iodine. It is thought to act via iodination (a form of halogenation) to oxidize lipids of the cell membrane and form salts with microbial proteins. Povidone-iodine is a broad-coverage antiseptic with microbicidal activity against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria (including spores and M. tuberculosis ), fungi, viruses, and protozoa. Additionally, this antimicrobial acts rapidly after application to the skin but has minimal residual activity, unlike chlorhexidine. In one study, as a facial antiseptic, it was considered a safer alternative to chlorhexidine. [5]

agent1_snippets.title: Skin Antiseptics -- Administration
agent1_snippets.content: Chlorhexidine-based scrubs are generally composed of 0.5% to 4% chlorhexidine in alcohol or aqueous base typically combined with detergent. Recent evidence suggests that it provides a bactericidal activity for 24 hours after a 2-minute application, making it a useful antiseptic for anticipated long procedures. However, as chlorhexidine can cause irreversible keratitis and middle ear ototoxicity (with sensorineural deafness), topical administration is relatively contraindicated for skin preparation involving or adjacent to the eye and conchal bowl. [5]

agent1_snippets.title: Disinfectants -- Issues of Concern -- Alcohol
agent1_snippets.content: Alcohol has been widely used for centuries as a disinfectant and antiseptic. However, due to their lack of sporicidal activity, alcohol-based products are not recommended for sterilization. Their broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity works through membrane damage and cell lysis. To enhance the efficacy of alcohol-based products, they are sometimes combined with emollients (to reduce evaporation time) or with other biocides, such as chlorhexidine, which remains on the skin longer than alcohol. [1]

agent1_snippets.title: Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing -- Procedures
agent1_snippets.content: Integral to susceptibility testing is the implementation of a robust quality assurance program. Quality assurance ensures that testing conditions and test systems performance are acceptable, limits variance in operator performance, and helps identify random errors. For susceptibility testing, measures are in place to ensure that media and reagents are of good quality, equipment is serviced correctly, staff is competent at performing testing, routine quality control (QC) testing is within acceptable limits, and patient results are reviewed before finalization. [13]

agent1_snippets.title: Skin Antiseptics -- Mechanism of Action -- Hydrogen Peroxide
agent1_snippets.content: Hydrogen peroxide is the simplest peroxide that forms highly reactive free radicals (hydroxyl radicals) that attack and oxidize essential cell components, including lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids. Although generally better against gram-positive bacteria, hydrogen peroxide also has a microbicidal effect on gram-negative bacteria, bacterial spores, fungi, and viruses with higher concentrations and longer contact times. In addition to its being a broad-spectrum agent, it has a rapid onset of action and low cost, which made it an attractive option in the past. While a common at-home remedy, the evidence argues against routine use as a surgical scrub or cleansing agent due to known cytotoxicity to keratinocytes. [6]

agent1_snippets.content: Commercial systems have their laboratory procedures according to the manufacturer’s guidelines. Generally, the direct colony suspension method is used for preparing inoculum from colonies grown within 18 to 24 hours, while the growth method can be used by incubating the inoculated broth (with fast-growing bacteria) within 2 to 6 hours. The usual McFarland standard for the turbidity of the inoculum is 0.5. [8]

agent1_snippets.title: Dakin Solution -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: English chemist Henry Dakin and French surgeon Alexis Carrel developed a Dakin solution to clean and rinse wounds. It was originally formulated as a battlefield wound antiseptic during World War I. [2] They devised the Carrel-Dakin treatment to irrigate wounds after surgical debridements were performed to preserve and save limbs. As a result of their work, Dakin solution has saved many lives (and limbs) since its creation. [3] The low cost and effectiveness of Dakin solution make this bactericidal antiseptic very popular in the healthcare field. It treats or prevents infections from cuts, abrasions, lacerations, skin ulcers, stage I to IV pressure ulcers, first- and second-degree burns, and even during surgery. [2] Its efficacy has been compared to negative pressure wound therapy for treating diabetic foot ulcer infections. [4] This is why Dakin solution (usually in a diluted form) continues to be used in practice today. [5]

agent1_snippets.title: Skin Antiseptics -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Antiseptics are chemical agents applied to the skin to reduce the microbial count and the risk of surgical site infections (SSIs). SSIs are defined as an infection occurring at the surgical site within 30 days of a procedure and represent one of the most common complications of dermatologic surgery. [1] The use of antiseptics dates back to the mid-1800s when Ignaz Semmelweis noted a dramatic decrease in periportal sepsis events with appropriate handwashing techniques, later reaffirmed with Lister’s use of carbolic acid, showing improved infection control and reduction in surgical morbidity. Antiseptic agents in dermatologic surgery commonly include chlorhexidine, povidone-iodine, chloroxylenol, isopropyl alcohol, hexachlorophene, benzalkonium chloride, and hydrogen peroxide. They should be used for most, if not all, procedures that enter the dermis of the skin or deeper. [2]

agent1_snippets.title: Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing -- Normal and Critical Findings
agent1_snippets.content: Antimicrobial susceptibility testing results are reported using interpretive criteria called clinical breakpoints. Essentially, a clinical breakpoint represents a value (such as MIC or zone diameter) that helps differentiate between organisms inhibited effectively or killed by a given antimicrobial, administered according to standard dosing (classified as susceptible), and those expected to be unaffected or resistant. [23]

agent1_snippets.title: Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing -- Procedures
agent1_snippets.content: A random error of up to 5% is acce­ptable. If encountere­d, laboratories should promptly redo quality control testing. When the error is resolve­d, regular testing can resume­. However, persiste­nt errors require imme­diate action to identify the root cause­, and patient results should not be re­ported until resolved, which may involve­ testing new quality control strains, evaluating additional mate­rials, or consulting with commercial antimicrobial susceptibility te­sting equipment manufacturers. Laboratories may ne­ed to switch to alternative te­sting methods until the issue is fixe­d. [10]

agent1_snippets.title: Dakin Solution -- Mechanism of Action
agent1_snippets.content: Dakin solution is a strong topical antiseptic widely used to clean infected wounds, ulcers, and burns. [6] Full-strength Dakin solution is usually diluted in water, depending on its intended use. A 0.5% solution of hypochlorite (containing approximately 5000 ppm free chlorine) is used for disinfecting areas contaminated with bodily fluids, including large blood spills (after the area has been cleaned with a detergent). Dilute Dakin solution (0.05% to 0.025%) can be used to irrigate, cleanse, or as a component in wet-to-dry dressings to treat or prevent skin and soft tissue infections. [5]

agent1_snippets.title: Skin Antiseptics -- Mechanism of Action -- Chloroxylenol
agent1_snippets.content: Chloroxylenol is a halophenol that is thought to act by microbial cell wall disruption and inactivation of cellular enzymes. It is likely bactericidal with broad coverage of gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria but less activity against M. tuberculosis , fungi, and viruses. Chloroxylenol is fairly slow-acting and has a minimal residual effect; despite this, it has shown a similar incidence of SSIs as chlorhexidine in a recent study.

agent1_snippets.title: Hand Hygiene -- Personnel
agent1_snippets.content: According to the CDC, established guidelines recommend that agents used for surgical hand scrubs should reduce microorganisms on intact skin in a substantial manner, contain a nonirritating antimicrobial preparation, have broad-spectrum activity, and be fast-acting and persistent. Studies have demonstrated that formulations containing 60% to 95% alcohol alone or 50% to 95% in combination with other products lower bacterial counts on the skin immediately post-scrub more effectively than other agents [15] .

agent1_snippets.title: Dakin Solution -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Dakin solution, also called Dakin fluid or Carrel-Dakin fluid, is a dilute sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) solution commonly known as bleach. [1] The sodium peroxide (NaO) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) mixture produces sodium hypochlorite. The main active agent in Dakin solution is created when the chlorine reacts with water in the environment to form hypochlorous acid (HClO). This hypochlorous acid produces a potent antibacterial effect in tissues. Neutrophils of the human immune system produce small amounts of hypochlorous acid inside phagosomes, which are used to digest bacteria and viruses. Unlike stronger germicidal solutions that contain carbolic acid or iodine, Dakin does not damage living cells or lose potency in the presence of blood serum. It has a solvent action on dead cells that hastens the separation of dead tissue from living tissue. Sodium hypochlorite solution must be buffered before use. The most commonly used substances are boric acid and sodium bicarbonate.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1388
agent2_snippets.content: Biguanides and amidines as antiseptics and disinfectants, nitrofuran derivatives, phenol and derivatives, and quaternary ammonium compounds are all distinct chemical classes that belong to the broader category of antiseptics and disinfectants. Each of these agents is used to eliminate or inhibit the growth of microorganisms on skin, surfaces, and medical equipment, providing essential antimicrobial protection in clinical and environmental settings. Although they differ in chemical structure and specific spectrum of activity, recognizing that they all function as antiseptics and disinfectants helps clinicians and infection‑control professionals select the most appropriate agent for a given patient‑care or hygiene application.

agent2_snippets.content: Phenol and its derivatives and quaternary ammonium compounds are both classified as types of antiseptics and disinfectants, belonging to the broader category of agents used to inhibit or destroy microorganisms on living tissues and inanimate surfaces. As members of the antiseptics and disinfectants group, these chemical families share the common purpose of preventing infection and maintaining hygiene in clinical and community settings.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1377
agent2_snippets.content: Chlorhexidin is linked to a wide range of medicated shampoos that incorporate chlorhexidine gluconate as a primary active moiety, often combined with additional antifungal or keratolytic agents. Specific formulations in which chlorhexidin has the active moiety include “chlorhexidine gluconate 10 MG/ML / ketoconazole 2.5 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine gluconate 20 MG/ML / ketoconazole 10 MG/ML / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine gluconate 20 MG/ML / ketoconazole 10 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo [Vital‑Ketodine],” “chlorhexidine gluconate 20 MG/ML / miconazole nitrate 20 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo [MiconaHex+Triz],” “chlorhexidine gluconate 30 MG/ML / climbazole 5 MG/ML / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine gluconate 40 MG/ML / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo,” and “chlorhexidine gluconate 40 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo [Vital‑Chlor].” In addition, chlorhexidin is identified as an ingredient in several broader shampoo products, namely “chlorhexidine / climbazole / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine / ketoconazole / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine / ketoconazole Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine / miconazole / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo,” and the simple “chlorhexidine Medicated Shampoo.” Across all these products, chlorhexidine (often in the form of chlorhexidine gluconate) serves as a key antiseptic component, while the accompanying agents such as ketoconazole, miconazole nitrate, climbazole, and salicyloyl phytosphingosine provide antifungal or keratolytic effects, creating combination therapies aimed at treating scalp infections, dandruff, and related dermatologic conditions.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 1058
agent2_snippets.content: Chlorhexidine (chlorhexidin) gluconate is the core antiseptic ingredient in a wide range of medicated shampoos, formulated at concentrations from 10 mg/mL to 40 mg/mL. These shampoos are combined with antifungal agents—ketoconazole (10 mg/mL) in products such as chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL (branded Keto‑C, Vital‑Ketodine) and chlorhexidine 23 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL (Ketochlor); miconazole nitrate (20 mg/mL) in chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL (Malaseb, MiconaHex+Triz); and climbazole (5 mg/mL) in chlorhexidine 30 mg/mL / climbazole 5 mg/mL. Anti‑inflammatory hydrocortisone (10 mg/mL) is incorporated in triple‑combination formulas such as chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / hydrocortisone 10 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL. The keratolytic and skin‑conditioning agent salicyloyl phytosphingosine (0.5 mg/mL) appears alone with chlorhexidine or in multi‑agent blends (e.g., chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL; chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL; chlorhexidine 30 mg/mL / climbazole 5 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL; chlorhexidine 40 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL). Additional antiseptic bases such as chloroxylenol (10 mg/mL) are used in formulations like chloroxylenol / miconazole / salicylic acid and chloroxylenol 10 mg/mL / miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL / salicylic acid 20 mg/mL. Other dose forms within the medicated shampoo category include miconazole‑only shampoos, urea 100 mg/mL shampoo, and chlorhexidine 40 mg/mL shampoo (Vital‑Chlor). Collectively, these varied dose forms illustrate how medicated shampoos can be tailored to provide antimicrobial, antifungal, anti‑inflammatory, and keratolytic therapy for dermatologic and otic conditions, with specific brand names and concentration combinations defining each product’s therapeutic focus.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 702
agent2_snippets.content: Medicated Shampoo is a therapeutic topical preparation offered in numerous dose‑form variants that are defined by specific active‑ingredient combinations and concentrations. The primary antiseptic moiety, chlorhexidine (often as chlorhexidine gluconate), appears in a wide range of formulations, including chlorhexidine 10 mg/mL, 20 mg/mL, 23 mg/mL, 30 mg/mL, and 40 mg/mL. Chlorhexidine is combined with antifungal agents such as ketoconazole (e.g., chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL, marketed as Ketochlor), miconazole nitrate (e.g., chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL, marketed as Malaseb or MiconaHex+Triz), and climbazole (e.g., chlorhexidine 30 mg/mL / climbazole 5 mg/mL). Anti‑inflammatory hydrocortisone is incorporated in multi‑agent products (e.g., chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / hydrocortisone 10 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL). Keratolytic and barrier‑supporting agents such as salicyloyl phytosphingosine (0.5 mg/mL) are added to several chlorhexidine‑based shampoos (e.g., chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL; chlorhexidine 30 mg/mL / climbazole 5 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL; chlorhexidine 40 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL). Stand‑alone chlorhexidine preparations (e.g., chlorhexidine 40 mg/mL, Vital‑Clor) are also listed. Additional medicated shampoos contain other active agents: miconazole alone or with salicylic acid (e.g., miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL / salicylic acid 20 mg/mL), chloroxylenol 10 mg/mL combined with miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL and salicylic acid 20 mg/mL, urea 100 mg/mL, and zinc pyrithione 3 mg/mL (the latter includes the inactive ingredient alpha‑tocopherol). These diverse formulations deliver antiseptic, antifungal, anti‑inflammatory, keratolytic, and moisturizing effects, targeting scalp infections, fungal dermatoses, inflammatory conditions, hyperkeratosis, and general dermatologic disorders.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 48
agent2_snippets.content: Ketoconazole is a widely used antifungal agent incorporated in numerous medicated shampoos (e.g., Nizoral 10 mg/mL and 20 mg/mL, Keto‑C, Vital‑Ketodine, Ketochlor, Pharmaseb, and generic ketoconazole shampoo). In many formulations it is combined with the broad‑spectrum antiseptic chlorhexidine gluconate (10–40 mg/mL), the topical corticosteroid hydrocortisone (5 mg/mL, 10 mg/mL, or hydrocortisone acetate 5 mg/mL), the keratolytic/skin‑conditioning agent salicyloyl phytosphingosine (0.5 mg/mL), chloroxylenol, or other antifungals such as miconazole nitrate (20 mg/mL) and climbazole (5 mg/mL). Representative combination products include chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / hydrocortisone 10 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL (Keto‑C, Vital‑Ketodine), chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL (Ketochlor), chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL (Malaseb, MiconaHex+Triz), chlorhexidine 30 mg/mL / climbazole 5 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL, chlorhexidine 40 mg/mL (with or without salicyloyl phytosphingosine), chloroxylenol / ketoconazole, chloroxylenol / miconazole nitrate / salicylic acid, and hydrocortisone‑only shampoos (5 mg/mL or acetate 5 mg/mL). Zinc pyrithione 19 mg/mL is also marketed as an antibacterial/anti‑seborrheic medicated shampoo. The “medicated shampoo” dosage form is a topical scalp preparation intended for fungal, bacterial, and seborrheic scalp disorders, delivering antiseptic, antifungal, anti‑inflammatory, and keratolytic actions. The extensive range of active ingredients and concentrations allows clinicians to tailor therapy to the specific microbial, fungal, inflammatory, or keratolytic needs of individual patients. From a safety standpoint, topical ketoconazole‑containing shampoos are contraindicated in patients receiving the systemic agents dihydroergotamine mesylate, dihydroergotamine tartrate, or ubrogepant due to potential adverse drug interactions.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 362
agent2_snippets.content: Chlorhexidin (chlorhexidine gluconate) is the core antiseptic active moiety in a broad range of medicated shampoos that are formulated to treat scalp infections, fungal overgrowth, bacterial colonization, and scaling dermatologic conditions. The product class “Medicated Shampoo” includes numerous dose forms that combine chlorhexidine at concentrations of 10 MG/ML, 20 MG/ML, 30 MG/ML, or 40 MG/ML with antifungal agents such as ketoconazole (2.5 MG/ML or 10 MG/ML), miconazole nitrate (20 MG/ML), climbazole (5 MG/ML), or with keratolytic and soothing agents such as salicyloyl phytosphingosine (0.5 MG/ML) and salicylic acid (18 MG/ML). Specific branded formulations include Keto‑C and Vital‑Ketodine (chlorhexidine 20 MG/ML + ketoconazole 10 MG/ML), Malaseb and MiconaHex+Triz (chlorhexidine 20 MG/ML + miconazole nitrate 20 MG/ML), and Vital‑Chlor (chlorhexidine 40 MG/ML). Additional combinations listed are chlorhexidine 20 MG/ML + ketoconazole 10 MG/ML + salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 MG/ML, chlorhexidine 30 MG/ML + climbazole 5 MG/ML + salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 MG/ML, chlorhexidine 40 MG/ML + salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 MG/ML, and chlorhexidine alone at 10 MG/ML, 20 MG/ML, or 40 MG/ML. The generic category also encompasses shampoos that pair chloroxylenol 10 MG/ML with miconazole nitrate 20 MG/ML and salicylic acid 20 MG/ML, as well as stand‑alone medicated shampoos containing miconazole, urea 100 MG/ML, or zinc pyrithione 19 MG/ML. The dose form “chlorhexidine / ketoconazole / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo” links the product class to this specific triple‑active combination. Collectively, these relationships illustrate the extensive use of chlorhexidine as a central antiseptic component across diverse medicated shampoo formulations designed to provide targeted antimicrobial, antifungal, keratolytic, and soothing effects for scalp and dermatologic therapy.
